EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The drought conditions that has been impacting the United States Virgin Islands continued during the month of July. The combination of the heat and drought is causing stress in vegetation, cattle and poultry. Veterinarians were asking to double the ration of water for the animals. Farmers are also reporting the death of several animals and are taking extra precautions to protect them from the heat and drought. A territorial Fire Ban is in effect for the islands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Long term drought conditions continued across St. Croix. Despite the island receiving some rain, the wells did not respond. Higher than normal temperatures also exacerbated conditions for farmers and local workers. The island stayed under Extreme Drought (D3) throughout the entire month.

CZ_NAME: ST. THOMAS / ST. JOHN AND ADJACENT ISLANDS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The drought conditions that has been impacting the United States Virgin Islands continued during the month of July. The combination of the heat and drought is causing stress in vegetation, cattle and poultry. Veterinarians were asking to double the ration of water for the animals. Farmers are also reporting the death of several animals and are taking extra precautions to protect them from the heat and drought. A territorial Fire Ban is in effect for the islands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme Drought (D3) conditions was observed for St. Thomas at the beginning of the month, but some rains relieved conditions to a Severe Drought (D2). However, the long term lack of rain made the island go again into D3 by the last third of the month. By this time, St. John also was categorized as having D2 conditions. Above than normal temperatures were also exacerbating conditions across the islands, making conditions difficult for farmers and outside workers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a very long streak of dry months across the Virgin Islands, shower frequency finally increased, in part due to Tropical Cyclone Tammy and an approaching trough. The rains were beneficial for the islands, improving the soil, well and agriculture conditions in these islands. However, it was not enough to completely eradicate the drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: St. Croix was under an Exceptional Drought (D4) at the beginning of the month, but with rains reported later in October, conditions improved to a Severe Drought (D2). Well levels remained low, but some improvement in soil and agriculture conditions are being observed.

CZ_NAME: ST. THOMAS / ST. JOHN AND ADJACENT ISLANDS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a very long streak of dry months across the Virgin Islands, shower frequency finally increased, in part due to Tropical Cyclone Tammy and an approaching trough. The rains were beneficial for the islands, improving the soil, well and agriculture conditions in these islands. However, it was not enough to completely eradicate the drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: With the rains received at the end of the month, Saint John is now free of any drought, while St. Thomas improved from Exceptional Drought (D4) to a Severe Drought (D2). Based on observations from the wells monitored by the USGS, well levels remain low at St. Thomas.

CZ_NAME: ST. THOMAS / ST. JOHN AND ADJACENT ISLANDS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The lack of rain throughout the beginning of the year yielded severe drought conditions across St. Thomas and Extreme Drought Conditions for St. Croix. The wells were being depleted of water, causing stress to vegetation, including agriculture. Additionally, a significant fire was reported in St. Croix.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe Drought (D2) conditions is reported by the Drought Monitor for St. Thomas. The water levels as the wells continue to decrease, while impacts on vegetation and agriculture is significant. A fire ban continues for the territory.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Exceptional Drought (D4) and Severe Drought (D2) were observed for St. Croix, and St. Thomas and Saint John, respectively. Some relief from the dry pattern was observed by the end of the month, but the islands remain under a rainfall deficit. In fact, the year to date deficits range from 5 to 6 inches at the international airports. Across St. Croix, the combination of the heat and drought is  causing stress in vegetation, cattle and poultry. Veterinarians are asking to double the ration of water for the animals, due to the drought stress. Farmers are also reporting the death of several animals and are taking extra precautions to protect them  from the heat and drought. A Territorial Fire Ban is in effect| for the islands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme (D3) to exceptional (D4) drought persisted across St. Croix. The lack of rains caused a significant fire across Frederiksted in agricultural lands. The extreme heat event also exacerbated the damage to cattle and poultry, and the lacks of rains affected the crops in the islands.  The island of St. Croix was briefly under a Exceptional Drought, but the rains at the end of the month improved conditions a little.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe Drought (D2) was persistent across St. Thomas and St. John. The well levels across the islands continue to decrease (less available water).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unsettled weather conditions prevailed due to an upper level cut-off low strengthening northwest of the islands. The divergence aloft was strong enough to support small hail over the Virgin Islands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A youtuber from St. Croix reported estimated pea sized hail in East End. The person said that there were two pulses when hail was heard hitting the windows during the storm. The hail left about 5 dents the side of a one cent coin in their car.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Frequent shower activity associated with a series of troughs moving across the northeastern Caribbean brought much needed relief from the long-term drought affecting the Virgin Islands. As a result, by the latter part of the month, Saint Croix was no longer under a Severe Drought. However, the rain was not as beneficial for St. Thomas, where Severe Drought conditions persisted into the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After observing significant rain showers, the drought in St. Croix improved from Severe (D2) to Moderate (D1). The wells were responding positively and the fire ban was lifted.

CZ_NAME: ST. THOMAS / ST. JOHN AND ADJACENT ISLANDS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Frequent shower activity associated with a series of troughs moving across the northeastern Caribbean brought much needed relief from the long-term drought affecting the Virgin Islands. As a result, by the latter part of the month, Saint Croix was no longer under a Severe Drought. However, the rain was not as beneficial for St. Thomas, where Severe Drought conditions persisted into the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions remained unchanged in St. Thomas through the month of November. Some precipitation fell into the islands, and the wells responded accordingly. However, the long term indices still indicate Severe Drought (D2) conditions prevailing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The lack of rain throughout the beginning of the year yielded severe drought conditions across St. Thomas and Extreme Drought Conditions for St. Croix. The wells were being depleted of water, causing stress to vegetation, including agriculture. Additionally, a significant fire was reported in St. Croix.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme Drought (D3) conditions is reported by the Drought Monitor for St. Croix. The water levels as the wells continue to decrease, while impacts on vegetation and agriculture is significant. A significant fire was reported on Frederiksted Agricultural Land. A fire ban continues for the territory.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Exceptional Drought conditions are again present across St. Thomas and St. Croix in the U.S. Virgin Islands, while a Severe Drought affects St. John. Rainfall deficits in the islands are greater than 10 inches. In fact, St. Croix have received less than 50% of the normal amount of rainfall since January 2023. These drought conditions are a big concern for farmers and residents. Soil and vegetation is showing signs of distress. Livestock and poultry have been negatively impacted, with several animals dying from the combination of drought and extreme heat. Farmers have been ordering hay and water in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Farmers are reporting  that rain is not penetrating the soil due to evaporation from heat and wind. The U.S.V.I. Department of Agriculture is buying and storing hay and providing water. Poultry farmers also report that they are concerned that guinea grass is sparce and poultry are not eating grain. There is a smaller number of egg production. These reports led the U.S. Drought Monitor to classify the island as Exceptionally Dry (D4).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The official stations along St. Thomas and St. Croix, as well as additional weather stations across these islands indicated heat indices above 108, or even 112 degrees in some days for at least a two hours period. These values met the criteria for Excessive Heat Warning across the Virgin Islands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS station at the airport, as well as the station in Cotton Valley frequently reported values that exceed the criteria of 108F or even 112F, for at least two hours, each day of the month.

CZ_NAME: ST. THOMAS / ST. JOHN AND ADJACENT ISLANDS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The official stations along St. Thomas and St. Croix, as well as additional weather stations across these islands indicated heat indices above 108, or even 112 degrees in some days for at least a two hours period. These values met the criteria for Excessive Heat Warning across the Virgin Islands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The official ASOS station, as well as several other unofficial stations across St. Thomas and St. John frequently reported heat indices values of at least 108F degrees for at least two hours. These stations reported values of 112 or more several days of the month, which meets the criteria for Excessive Heat Warning.

CZ_NAME: ST. THOMAS / ST. JOHN AND ADJACENT ISLANDS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Exceptional Drought conditions are again present across St. Thomas and St. Croix in the U.S. Virgin Islands, while a Severe Drought affects St. John. Rainfall deficits in the islands are greater than 10 inches. In fact, St. Croix have received less than 50% of the normal amount of rainfall since January 2023. These drought conditions are a big concern for farmers and residents. Soil and vegetation is showing signs of distress. Livestock and poultry have been negatively impacted, with several animals dying from the combination of drought and extreme heat. Farmers have been ordering hay and water in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Farmers are reporting that ponds are evaporating due to high heat. Planting has been reduced. Livestock farmers are separating males and females to reduce births due to the lack of forage land. They are also buying grain and hay for the animals. These conditions led the U.S. Drought monitor to classify the island of St. Thomas under Exceptional Drought (D4), while a Severe Drought (D3) was also introduced to St. John by the middle of the month.

CZ_NAME: ST. THOMAS / ST. JOHN AND ADJACENT ISLANDS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The lack of rain caused the lost of soil moisture as well levels continue to decrease. This is impacting vegetation, which is showing signs of stress. The access to water is also impacting the agriculture sectors.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The lack of rains and dry soils, as well as current impacts to agriculture and vegetation have lead to the continuation of a Severe Drought (D2) across Saint Thomas. Saint John remained free of drought.
